new_issues_this_run.description: KILLSHOT-GAIA underdeveloped: Section 4.3 is ~100 words vs ~1,500 for Kill-Shot #1 and ~500 for Kill-Shot #2. No anticipated objection, no specific numerical comparison between dome prediction (single ~0.45 arcsec wobble for all stars) and Gaia measurement (parallaxes from 0.768 arcsec down to microarcseconds), and no engagement with the dome's actual defense. A dome defender would dismiss this kill-shot as an unsupported assertion.

KILLSHOT-GAIA strawman: Section says 'A local sun 20,000 km away cannot produce the observed parallactic shifts' — but the dome does not claim parallax comes from a local sun. The dome's actual claim (WIN-017) is firmament wobble (20m lateral wobble → ~0.45 arcsec). We are attacking a claim the dome does not make. The correct critique is that firmament wobble predicts a fixed parallax for ALL stars, whereas Gaia measures a continuous distance-dependent range from 0.768 arcsec to microarcseconds.

KILLSHOT-GAIA missing refraction defense: Section does not mention aetheric refraction, even though Section 4.9 explicitly lists WIN-017 (parallax) as depending on it. A dome defender reading only the kill-shot section would invoke refraction immediately. Should cross-reference Section 4.9 and explain why position-dependent refraction cannot reproduce the per-star parallax variation (refraction depends on observer position, not star distance — so all stars in the same direction would show the same refraction-induced shift).

KILLSHOT-GAIA missing New Horizons parallax experiment: NASA's New Horizons spacecraft (April 2020) measured stellar parallax from a 7-billion-km baseline. Proxima Centauri shifted by ~32.4 arcsec, Wolf 359 by ~15.7 arcsec — directly visible in before/after images. This is physically impossible if stars are on a dome: the observation baseline (7 billion km) is ~800,000 times larger than the dome's firmament height (9,086 km), and the measurement was made from beyond any hypothetical dome, eliminating aetheric refraction as an escape hatch. This is the strongest single piece of evidence against the firmament-wobble hypothesis.

KILLSHOT-GAIA missing adjacent-star parallax argument: In any small patch of sky, Gaia measures stars with parallaxes differing by factors of 100-1000 (e.g., a nearby star at 0.5 arcsec and a background star at 0.001 arcsec). Any dome motion — rigid wobble, flexible undulation, or refraction-induced apparent shift — would affect all objects in the same direction similarly. The per-star parallax variation, perfectly correlated with independently determined distance, is impossible to reproduce with any dome mechanism. This 'differential parallax in the same field of view' argument is devastating and missing from the section.

KILLSHOT-GAIA missing scale comparison: Proxima Centauri's parallax-derived distance (4.02 × 10^13 km) is approximately 4.7 billion times the dome's maximum firmament height (8,537 km). This ratio is never stated and would be viscerally compelling. Section should note: 'The nearest star is 4.7 billion dome-heights away — the dome model must explain how light from 4.7 billion firmament heights above the surface reaches observers through a copper dome at 8,537 km.'

KILLSHOT-GAIA: Section refers to 'The Gaia space telescope' — Gaia is technically a space observatory or astrometric satellite (ESA nomenclature: 'space observatory'). Minor imprecision; 'ESA Gaia space observatory' or simply 'Gaia' would be more accurate.

KILLSHOT-GAIA missing from DOCX/PDF: The Gaia kill-shot appears as a standalone section in the HTML (Section 4.3) but build-doc-v4.js has no corresponding section. The Word/PDF download is missing one of the six kill-shots entirely. Needs a new section added to build-doc-v4.js after the GPS kill-shot section.

internal_issues.description: Finding field overstates: 'Working geophysicists deliberately avoid this noisy frequency band' conflates AMT dead band (1-5 kHz) with Schumann inter-harmonic minimum. Geophysicists use this band; it just has lower SNR.

suggested_patch.find: Working geophysicists deliberately avoid this noisy frequency band
suggested_patch.replace: The ~12 Hz inter-harmonic trough has reduced natural signal power, not a useful telluric peak

internal_issues.description: WIN-006 detail_evidence states 'roughly 9 km per year before 1990' — this is the 1900-1990 endpoint average. Decade-by-decade rates in 1975-1990 were 12-18 km/yr; the scientific literature cites 10-15 km/yr for the pre-acceleration period.

suggested_patch.find: westward drift at roughly 9 km per year before 1990
suggested_patch.replace: westward drift at roughly 10–15 km per year before 1990 (NOAA historical records; rates reached ~15–18 km/yr in the 1975–1990 decade)

internal_issues.description: WIN-006 verdict_text doesn't challenge the dome's 0.0466 deg/yr rate, which cannot be reproduced from NOAA NP.xy data by any standard derivation.

suggested_patch.find: Both models fit the same NOAA data equally well.
suggested_patch.replace: Both models fit the same NOAA data equally well. The dome's claimed angular drift rate of 0.0466 deg/yr cannot be reproduced from the NOAA NP.xy dataset by any standard metric (great-circle angular rate gives ~0.010–0.012 deg/yr; longitude regression gives ~0.038 deg/yr) — the dome's metric is undefined, rendering the claimed rate unverifiable.

internal_issues.description: WIN-026 rebuttal lacks quantitative geometry for anticrepuscular ray argument. A smart defender could argue we're hand-waving.

suggested_patch.find: A local sun at 5,733 km could not produce rays converging at both horizons simultaneously.
suggested_patch.replace: A local sun at 5,733 km could not produce rays converging at both horizons simultaneously. Simple geometry: two shadow rays passing through cloud gaps separated by 5 km at 10 km altitude would subtend a ~0.05° angular difference at ground level — a difference that only widens as the observer turns away from the sun. Reconvergence at the anti-solar point is the geometric signature of a source at effectively infinite distance (≫ 5,733 km), consistent with a sun at 150 million km.

internal_issues.description: WIN-026 cites atoptics.co.uk as only source. NASA Earth Observatory provides authoritative photographic documentation with scattering physics analysis.

suggested_patch.replace: See Atmospheric Optics and NASA Earth Observatory for photographic documentation and scattering physics analysis.

internal_issues.description: WIN-035 says dome 'offers no mechanism for why the decay rate is ~25 nT/yr' — this contradicts the earlier stated rate of ~28-30 nT/yr, and misses that the dome does assert a mechanism (aetheric field geometry, no equations).

suggested_patch.find: it offers no mechanism for why the decay rate is ~25 nT/yr rather than any other value.
suggested_patch.replace: it offers no mechanism for why the decay rate is ~28–30 nT/yr rather than any other value. The dome claims 'aetheric field geometry' explains faster African cell decay due to the cell's position relative to the pole axis — but provides no equations connecting disc_radius (20,015 km) or firmament parameters to the observed rate. The mechanism is asserted, not derived.

internal_issues.description: WIN-035 lists WMM2025 and INTERMAGNET as separate data sources, which could suggest independent confirmations. WMM2025 incorporates INTERMAGNET data.

suggested_patch.find: Both the WMM2025 (World Magnetic Model) and the dome model extrapolate this same trend from the same INTERMAGNET station data.
suggested_patch.replace: Both the WMM2025 (World Magnetic Model), which synthesizes data from INTERMAGNET observatories worldwide, and the dome model extrapolate this same trend from the same underlying observatory measurements.

internal_issues.description: WIN-038 attributes 'quarter-wave formula f = c/(4H)' to the dome without clarifying it's the dome's own formula (from WIN-029 back-derivation) applied forward to the dome's cavity.

suggested_patch.find: The quarter-wave formula f = c/(4H_eff) gives predicted fundamentals

internal_issues.description: WIN-044 evidence is thin (4 sentences). Should add detail_extra with FSF formula, station values, and acknowledgment that this is one of few WINs genuinely derived from dome geometry.

suggested_patch.replace: The Firmament Scaling Function (FSF) formula is constructed from dome parameters as FSF(r) = H(r) / H(0) = exp(−r/λ_g), where H(0) = H₀ = 8,537 km and λ_g = 8,619 km. This makes WIN-044 unusual among the 67 WINs: it is one of only a handful genuinely derived from dome geometry rather than adopted post-hoc from observational data. The curmudgeon's code analysis tags confirm: derives_from_dome = true. However, 'derived from model geometry' is not the same as 'confirmed by independent data.' The eclipse predictions (E001–E009) that would test FSF against INTERMAGNET eclipse measurements remain pending (August 12, 2026 eclipse). Until those results are in, the formula is a mathematical consistency check within the model's own assumptions — not an independent confirmation.

internal_issues.description: WIN-044 detail blocks are thin. Same as ISS-103; addressed by detail_extra patch above.
internal_issues.note: Addressed by ISS-103 patch (detail_extra content added).

internal_issues.description: Curmudgeon noted OPEN-015 not mentioned in WIN-057. Current detail_evidence already mentions OPEN-015 twice: 'the dome's model still cannot handle equatorial routes at all (OPEN-015 concedes near-equatorial cities fall beyond the range)' and 'OPEN-015 acknowledges that near-equatorial cities below ~12° latitude fall beyond the range of the V13 model.' Issue is already fixed.
internal_issues.note: ALREADY FIXED — WIN-057 detail_evidence already mentions OPEN-015 twice. Recommend closing ISS-375 as fixed.

internal_issues.description: WIN-057 detail_extra is null. Substantial analysis potential: free parameter count, OPEN-015 limitation, only six cited routes.

suggested_patch.replace: Parameter accounting: Standard geodesy (WGS84 ellipsoid) uses two derived parameters — semi-major axis a = 6,378,137 m and flattening f = 1/298.257 — both determined independently from satellite orbit perturbations, gravimetry, VLBI, and SLR. The V13 dome coordinate system uses at least four fitted parameters: H₀ (firmament apex height), λ_g (firmament decay constant), EW angular scale 0.9941, and equatorial ring radius 14,105 km. More parameters, less accuracy (6.2% RMSE vs sub-0.01% for Vincenty geodesy) — the textbook signature of overfitting. Evidence coverage: the 6.2% RMSE is computed from only six cherry-picked intercontinental routes. The dome does not report accuracy for short SH-internal routes, cross-equatorial routes under 1,000 km, or NH routes that were not in the training set. Sample size of six is insufficient to establish a 6.2% RMSE as a reliable global performance figure. OPEN-015 acknowledgment: The dome's coordinates page explicitly concedes Singapore (1.35°N) and similar near-equatorial cities 'fall beyond the equatorial ring' — approximately 40% of the world's population lives in the tropics below ±12° latitude. A coordinate system that fails for 40% of inhabited locations is not a replacement for standard geodesy.

internal_issues.description: WIN-057 and WIN-058 are conflated in build-doc-v4.js (prose section). This is a prose-only fix in build-doc-v4.js — not patchable to wins.json. Deferring to prose section refactor.
internal_issues.note: DEFERRED — prose-only fix in build-doc-v4.js (conflation of WIN-057/058 in the Word document). Not patchable to wins.json. Will require manual edit of the DOCX builder.

internal_issues.description: WIN-058 detail_extra is null. The evidence field is very long but detail_extra could add a concise summary.

suggested_patch.replace: The 0.9941 EW scale factor can be compared to the globe's flattening f = 1/298.257 to illustrate the epistemological gap: the globe's flattening is derived from Earth's rotation rate and mass distribution, independently confirmed by four methods (satellite orbits, gravimetry, VLBI, SLR), with each method giving consistent results to 8 significant figures. The dome's 0.9941 is the output of a least-squares minimization on six WGS84-derived route distances, with no physical derivation, no independent confirmation, and no theoretical prediction prior to observing the data. The dome's own AI context lists 'EW scale 0.9941 physical interpretation' as an open question — the model concedes the parameter has no theoretical basis. This is the difference between a measured physical quantity and a curve-fitting artifact.

internal_issues.description: WIN-064 detail_evidence is thin. Misses: (1) flat disc produces no angular shadow zone at all — only simple attenuation; (2) IRIS/GEOSCOPE network consistency; (3) dome site has NO discussion of P-wave shadow zones anywhere in raw-text.

suggested_patch.find: Claiming this as evidence for a flat disc is self-defeating: the shadow zone calculations assume spherical wave propagation through concentric spherical layers.
suggested_patch.replace: Claiming this as evidence for a flat disc is self-defeating: the shadow zone calculations assume spherical wave propagation through concentric spherical layers. On a flat disc 20,015 km in radius, there is no spherical liquid core to refract P-waves — a flat disc would produce simple geometric attenuation with distance, not a sharp angular shadow zone with PKP re-emergence beyond 140°. The dome provides no flat-disc mechanism that predicts these specific cutoff angles. The IRIS Global Seismographic Network (143 stations) and GEOSCOPE (30 stations) have mapped this shadow zone consistently for decades; P-wave arrival times, amplitudes, and slownesses all fit the spherical Earth model to better than 1% precision. Notably, the dome's own source files contain no discussion of P-wave shadow zones at any of its seven pages — WIN-064 was added in V51.0 without any accompanying explanation of how a flat disc predicts this observation.

internal_issues.description: Dome's raw-text files contain NO discussion of P-wave shadow zones. WIN-064 was added in V51.0 with no explanation of the dome's mechanism for this observation.
internal_issues.note: Addressed by ISS-396 patch (note about no dome source discussion added to detail_evidence).
